author: @tonybee5605
text: I have a low voltage system at home, so I basically have a 12v system running a 24 inch TV for general use that draws around 30 watts and I have a 12v radio in the house and the workshop add in a few 12v led lights and I can run most of my immediate needs from the solar / batteries (lead acid) for free, then swap to the large tv and mains lighting when desired, I also run a log burning stove in the cooler months, which with a flat bottomed stove kettle supplies me with hot water for drinks and various other small tasks, I don't run an inverter at all as the supply cables need to be big and of course in the UK winter time, solar output is vastly reduced so even a small inverter would seriously damage my 12v reserves, so i just plug standard electrical items into the mains. but a free unlimited tv (recently retired so the Tv may be on 16 hours a day) and lighting seriously reduces the electric bill.

This can perhaps simplify selection on certain appliances whether buying DC or AC powered.

I'm using Oscal PM6000 - 3600wh master unit which can be connected to 15 more additional extended BR3600 - 3600wh sub unit. Make it a maximum 56KW.  It's expandable.  PM6000 has 3 x AC120V/20A + 1 x AC240V/30A output, plus 5V/12V and 12V cigarette socket output.  All sub units are purely lithium batter power storage, no inverter, MPPT.   Plug your solar panels maximum 2400w to each master unit, you will have both AC and DC power whenever you need them.

author: @ghostwheelppk
text:
Awesome video, Robert!   It occurs to me that if the solar panels are [an 800 watt array for example] 200 watts each then in parallel the Voltage would be 12 volts but the currents (200/12)would add ( 16.67 x 4 = 66.67 amps.). In series the Voltage would be (12+12+12+12 =) 48 volts and the amperage would be (200/12) = 16.67.     

Although there is less loss from converting voltages up and down, wouldn't the wire gauge requirements be different for moving 67 amps than for 17 amps? 

If we take a sample length of 20 meters through an 8AWG cable,  the 67 amps at 12 volts the voltage drop would be 5.38 volts with 360 watts lost as heat.   Final voltage 6.62 V and 443.4 Watts delivered.

If the wire size is increased to 00 AWG,  The Voltage drop goes to 0.84 Volts and 56.4W watts lost (out of 800) for 747.6 Watts transmitted.

If we use the same 20 meters of 8AWG cable and move 17 amps at 48 volts  the voltage drop would be 1.37 volts with a power loss of 23.2 watts to heat with 792.8 watts delivered.

For the same amount of power generated (by the solar panels) lower voltage means higher amperage. That increased amperage needs more conductor volume ( length x cross section) to deliver a comparable amount of power. If the wire diameter is not large enough the electrical energy is lost as heat.

Does that make sense?

replies.author: @TheTubejunky
replies.text: yes you're correct and that is why micro inverters are a thing. The voltage losses are far far less and the signal is much much simpler to match downstream without too much impedance when weather or environmental changes occur.

author: @steveroberts1861
text: 12v DC makes sense in these times. As you say, most appliances have so many inefficient and RF noisy step-down converters. Caravans, I believe, use 12v throughout. In a large house installation, you'd potentially have losses due to distances. I wonder how you'd deal with noise coming onto the 12v supply from all those smaller converters like LED downright?

author: @neildee9834
text:
Depending on your battery setup that issue is largely resolved by the cross wiring of the main large cables joining the batteries. 
For instance,  i still (until they wear out) use 3 x 130ah AGM lead acids. Tbe connection leads both join and cross, so that no one battery is over or under loaded, or charged.
As most peple are now going the Lithium route as the technology matures, one battery usually suffices, and folk are doing great things with hybrid battery systems nowadays. 
12v boating group is a great Facebook community full of cleverer people that i!

author: @neildee9834
text:
Having had a campervan and a couple of boats, living on board onefor a  couple of years, the fixation of 230v bothered me too.
Apart from voltage drop and tge requirements for heavier cables, there is almost no downside.
My current live-aboard boat runs happily for eight or nine months of the year off 400w of solar.
Everything is super efficient; 12v fridge, led lights, pumps, diesel heater, sound bar, tv and recharging all 12v.
If i really must have my coffee machine on in the morning, i switch on the 2000w inverter for 230v for a few minutes, and promptly switch it off again.
The same goes for the air fryer and microwave. 
Only in mid November to March do i struggle for power, but a short cruise every few days, or maybe an hour blast with a generator sorts that.
I switch my fridge off as its generally just as cold outside in December and January so leave stuff out on deck in a cool box!  😂
Efficiency is key, with as little stepping up or down as possible. Most items wire straight in, with perhaps a cheap voltage regulator or buck converter for protection or small steps up or down accordingly. 
24v makes even more sense if you can source the items.

author: @douglasheld
text: 6:56 When it comes through the hole, you should droop the cables down and back up into the hole, and fix them that way. So moisture dripping on the cables doesn't lead inside.

author: @rafalklepinski7372
text: Spot on about the inverters. Even if your inverter is 90% efficient, and the AC-DC converter in your device is also 90% efficient, that's an 81% overall efficiency. You lose almost 20% of your energy! I'm currently using boost and buck converters as needed. No inverter, no AC. If you're super savvy and design your own circuits you can design a buck converter right into your device with a wide voltage input range. But that's usually not possible for off-the-shelf devices.

text: I completely agree with building a 12v system and thats what I have on my shed! I made a mistake though, I bought a big 450w house panel from CityPlumbing for £70 which is incredible value, it outputs 40v. I originally bought a controller similar to yours which said its supports 12v-48v batteries and upto 100vdc solar input however the small print then tells you, the lower the battery voltage, the lower the maxium solar input can be. For a 12v battery the max voltage from the panels was about 24v. So putting 40v wasn't going to be safe. You shouldn't run into that problem with your smaller panels in parallel. I ended up buying a bigger controller, all working well now.

author: @collie147
text:
You could go with a universal DC voltage at a high enough level, say what they used for phone lines at 50v (also the limit of what's required for a qualified electrician to wire).  Maybe seeing as it was used for phone wires maybe use the modern equivalent, and use cat 6 cable. While you're at it why not put ethernet on there too for computers and IP phones.  As it's got ethernet too you could call it power over eth.... Oh.  That's already been invented. 

I've a PoE at home and it is really useful for all the small electronics. I've even got it on a redundant power supply so if the power goes in the area (which it does 4 or 5 times a year) I've still got internet, alarm, phones and some home monitoring equipment (PoE for Arduino and raspberry pi are fairly cheap too). But as you say there is a whole market for 12v systems (batteries, BMS etc.), particularly for caravans which means it's easily adapted. Initially I had a 12v battery system and stepped it up for 48v for PoE but over time it became more efficient to go to 48 and step down for those 12v options.

author: @shanefthomas
text: When you run an inverter  for 230v ac there is an idle draw, typically 1%. My 1kw inverter is 10w (24*10=240w every day. ) ( 6kw inverter 50w idle ( 50*24 = 1200w lost every day ) My 300W inverter is 4w idle so on a 12V 100Ah Lifepo4 battery  can power a efficient fridge,  12V fan And lights for a day. The 6kw inverter would flatten the battery in a day without doing any work. So having a mixed system where you can turn the inverter off is great if solar is limited. A real winner is replacing a 230vac 16 inch stand Fan  with a common type 7555 12V motor. The common 3 speed ac fans draw 35 to 55w the 12dc motor draws 2 to 24W and PWM speed controler is brilliant. Motor $3 PWM 1$ USD ( Direct from China pricing for Asia ) . The small 12V fan motor sits inside the old ac motor in place of the armature. See utube vids from Asia.

author: @duncancremin1708
text:
People get so hot under the collar about the series/parallel argument. The ONLY advantage to the series set up, in a properly designed system with the correct charge controller, is you get away with less copper in the cables from roof to charge controller. That’s it. Given adequate girth, both setups behave exactly the same, in ideal conditions.
But, here’s the kicker. When things are NOT ideal, like when one panel is shaded, or simply dirty, series wiring results in the whole system being limited to whatever the maximum current from the shaded panel is. Parallel wiring will still allow the unimpeded panels to produce their peak current, so in real world conditions, if you have room and the budget for heavy cables, parallel gives better long term results.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i agree they do get hot under the collar for no good reason and I like your argument - thanks for sharing mate

author: @ETJAKEOC
text:
You hit the nail on the head with the 12V rail system idea. There are so many 12V electronics available that can utilize this power source, worse case, you can always scale up to 24/36/48V too depending on your load needs, and slap in buck/boost converters to generate the 5/9/12/19.5V or whatever you may need, digital logic/USB, smoke detectors, audio amplifiers and LED lights, charging laptops and running small desktop PC's, all these things are completely possible via these sorts of setups, and the best part, that most people overlook 99% of the time; This setup is a cheap mans UPS. Who needs to buy a UPS module just for a PC to keep it from loosing data during a brownout, when you can have a battery supply that keeps it running for more than long enough to properly save the state and shut the machine down.

Fun fact, the USA is looking into adapting the grid from HVAC to EHVDC, due to all the renewables, since AC phase matching is a female dog 😂 DC of course is much simpler, either series or parallel, the power adds up, there's no phase magic to deal with. EHVDC is also much more energy efficient over distance vs AC due to impedance. The inductance and capacitance of the mains eats AC power that we don't even think about on our day to day scale, but is the bane of electrical grid engineers, and this is why the idea to switch to DC over AC is more enticing, as at high enough DC voltages, the resistance of the conductors becomes negligible, and it actually becomes more efficient to push the DC down the length than it does AC.

author: @simonduffy99
text: Please link to a genuine 12v 2000w pure sine wave inverter that's £50. While I appreciate that a 12v system benefits from having lots of appliances being available, the same can be said for 240v/110v AC. The AC appliances tend to be significantly cheaper, and there's much more choice of appliances, and at various price points. Yes, there's a trade off with loses in conversion, but your house is probably already wired for 240v, so there's no need to add all the additional hardware required to run DC currents around. Solar panels are relatively inexpensive, so if space permits, additional generation capacity would make up the the losses.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I agree - to a degree! -  that's why I haven't done my house - but this is a shed and allows me the opportunity to experiment.

author: @pauldymott8991
text: Great Brackets Rob.  I know you know, but it's worth pointing out how correct you are mate.  On our boat we have a 12v kettle and heater (bought from halfords for intended use in cara/vans), a 12v fridge freezer, 12v tv and loads of 12v lighting.  No inverter,  and therefore no loss for any of those.  We do have an electric blanket for the winter that needs a 150w inverter which hardly taxes our battery bank.  There are also loads of surface mount usb sockets that run from 12v.  As you say, what isn't powered by usb these days.  Keep it up mate.

text:
I was for quiet long thinking about idea of running DC through house because everything runs DC anyway, glad to see video about idea.
Interesting fact is that most modern equipment that have computers in them actually run DC.
How they run is usually rectifying wall AC and then using DC to power invertor that controls motors, things like fridges and washing machines.

author: @ericblenner-hassett3945
text: One thing to look into is how to connect with the batteries.   I have read in several locations that a bus bar system with one end to power,  ground " bus bar connected to the physical opposite end.  This allegedly has the advantage of not having power enter and exit one of a row of batteries.   The other thing to watch is the amperage of connected devices as they will need bigger wires so as not to become heating elements in the system.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the choices in connection are parallel or serial mate - in serial you are always going to have a first battery - so to speak. Your point about the wiring is spot on but in house wiring 1mm csa wire or even 1.5mm csa which is more common - is often used for lighting -1mm is rated to carry about 6 amps at 12v that's 72 watts and 1,5mm is 14A, 12V 168w - not a lot for old style lighting where a dim bulb was often 40-60w. But these days LED lighting is far more common and I have long since replaced all my old bulbs with LED lamps with the same lumens at 4-8w.

author: @brymstoner
text: interesting considerations. i'd probably opt for the on-demand choice of inverting only when needed, simply for the greater level of control you'd have over the circuit. and you could go with heavier gauge welders cable and fuses to buffer the panels ability to overproduce and fend off a bit of resistance. something i learned the hard way with my first charge controller and cables - my 1st 400W panel used to pull in ~700W constant on a good stretch of sun!

author: @Dave_D.
text: In my, admittedly limited, experience, the inexpensive 'pure sine wave' inverters do not really produce a pure sine wave.  I have one as a backup and it makes my PC hum like crazy!  My quite expensive inverter does not.  I chose not to try to run my refrigerator w/ the cheap one.  It worked OK for some things, but others, not so much.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: to be honest mate - no so called pure sine wave inverter produces a pure sine wave - they all use switching - the only real difference is the number of times they switch - cheap ones switch about 3 times the more expensive ones switch at rates about 5 times - but they all are stepped wave functions - calling it pure sine wave is really just marketing

author: @bartronicsecurity
text:
After having spent many years playing with different voltages on various sites the idea of voltage turns out to be 24 volts. The reason is simple you can get 24 volt fridges, deep freezers, kettles, microwaves, washing machines, TVs, hair dryers, vacuum cleaners, in fact almost anything you want on 24 volts. It seems that international lead 24 volts the accepted as the emergency voltage used by the military and also for medical field hospitals. Campers and trucks usie 24 volts almost exclusively when using DC. There are plenty 24 to 12 volt down converters for 12 volt products. 24 to 19 volt converters used for notebooks are available and used by truckers and campus. Almost anything you need is available on 24 volts. Next the fridges and deep freezers using 24 volts only use around 5,0 or 6 amps compare to 12 to 14 amps for the 12 volt versions. So running a 12 volt meant having to use very heavy cable verse 24 volts much lighter cable requirements and far less effect with current voltage drops on the cable.
Next on one site where we used 24 volts we did not even rewire the house. We simply change the plug outlets do you have fuel cigarette lighter sockets so that standard 24 volt products could plug straight into these sockets. The wiring was standard 16 to a 20 amp cables which was more than capable of carrying the current of these 24 volt products. A deep freezer and fridge together used 12 amps which work quite happily on the one socket in the kitchen. All we did was convert the DB board to run 24 volts instead of 220 volts. 24 volts really did turnout to be the best all-round solar off grid viability.

Ions are subject to gravity and therefore gravity flow 😊😊😊😊I'm happy I had to tell you

author: @Иван_Абрамов
text: I disagree about the voltage of 12 volts, even 110 is not enough. The problem with low voltage is in huge currents under any serious load, for example, in Russia, even 3-phase voltages of 400 volts are used, which allows you to connect powerful electric stoves or machines. Calculate what the current will be if you connect a standard electric kettle to 12 volts, 2200 watts, current 1803 amperes, or, for example, an electric stove 5000-10000 watts, current 833 amperes, and what will be the losses on the wires, what kind of wires will these be, moreover, it is more difficult to work with thick and rough wires, and more difficult in all senses.

replies.author: @fleaniswerkhardt4647
replies.text: A standard kettle  running at 240v is rated around 2200W so that is using roughly 2200/240 =  ~9.2 amps. If it was 12v for the same load then the current would be 9.2 x 20 = 184 amps wouldn't it?

author: @mikehardy7060
text: You like redundancy as do I.  Ultimate redundancy in your system, 4 solar panels, 4 batteries, would be to connect each panel to it's own battery via it's own controller and then connect the batteries in parallel as already planned.  you mentioned in the previous video about having more than one controller.  it would give an easy way to expand the system should the option be needed or just preferred, one panel, one controller, one battery and on pair of leads to connect the battery to the rest.  even better would be to used consumer unit in reverse to connect each controller to it's own suitable circuit breaker to the busbar and the "input" would then be the output.  if desired you could then connect that to the input of another consumer unit and use that in the conventional way.  you would then have the normal individual circuits plus the ability to isolate each panel/battery set without the others for maintenance etc.

replies.author: @glencahalin4786
replies.text:
Connecting the batteries in parallel connects the chargers in parallel.

An important thing to do with this system is, as these are lead acid batteries, turn the equalisation off on all but one of the charge controllers. Else not only would they get that done twice as frequently as they should, itself not good, but there could also be timing clashes of when it happens.

author: @grendel1960a
text: a 12v system makes a lot of sense, (though there are some advantages to running a 24V system and droping the voltage as necessary for the specific output) a variable voltage output board with up to 40V input is cheap as chips, but will only give a max output slightly less than the input, so starting at 24V you can output 19V for a laptop for example. but if you want 12v, then go for it. after all every mobile home, and every boat is normally running a 12v system, and the parallels are there to work with, you can manage just about anything from 12V. replacing batteries that fail is easy (just pop along to halfords, or a boat chandlery). my unimat lathe actually runs on a 24V electric scooter motor now and that has 100% use cycle unlike its original 240V motor (40/60 use cycle) the only issue i had converting my workshop to solar, was the big lathe, I didnt spec my inverter big enough for that.

author: @t77chevy
text: you don't wire solar panels in series or parallel based on battery bank voltage , for example you said if you wired your four panels in series that's for a 48 volt battery and that's false ,  you wire the solar panels for the mppt you're using , those look like 100 watt solar panels and their voc " open circuit voltage " will be around 20 - 22 volts , this is the spec you use to wire the panels for the mppt , lets say you have a victron 100/50 mppt , the 100 is for max voltage from solar panels and the 50 is max charge amperage to battery bank , 22 x 4 =88 volts which is well inside the 100 volt limit , 50 x 12=600 watts possible to battery bank at 12 volts so good fit " its actually more as charging 12 volt batteries the voltage is above 12 volts , how much above depends on battery type , the solar panels working voltage vmp is around 17-19.5 volts on average for 100 watt panels and amperage around 4.5-5.5  amps , lets use 19 vmp for this example would have an amperage at roughly 5.3 amps , in series the 19 x 4=76 volts  at 5.3 amps and in parallel it would be 5.3 x4=21.2 amps at 19 volts , you would require far larger and more expensive pv wire wire for 19 volts and 21.2 amp configuration than a 76 volt 5.3 amp configuration to not have high transmission losses , same reason everyone says 48 volt systems as 1/4 the amperage so far cheaper components / less losses when you're not using the 12 volt dc straight from the battery bank " I do this with some 12 volt refrigerators and led lights myself '"and run 48 volt system for ac conversion to power 120 volt ac " usa appliances "

replies.author: @dennisolsson3119
replies.text: I couldn't muster the energy to read that whole sentence, but Rob said in a precious video he was going for PWM, not MPPT. Also a redundant system with multiple regulators.

replies.author: @t77chevy
replies.text: @dennisolsson3119 while I did miss the pwm part of the video as I do tend to multitask that decision alone defeats the whole purpose of using 12 volts dc directly to save power , pwm are only 70-80% efficient while mppt are 95-99%  , in fact he could easily run an mppt and an ac inverter and lose less power total vs the pwm and direct battery usage but would easily save power from going mppt and direct battery from there , each of those regulators will also lower efficiency as each one will also consume power to regulate , how much completely depends on their design

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: It's not based on battery bank voltage mate - that's just a coincidence. This particular equation has two sides. Panel being one side and batteries being the other - the link is the controller and in practice all you really do is follow the controller specs.

author: @offgridwanabe
text: AH yes the endless debate to parallel or series your panels as long as you have the wire size correct the parallel system should be more economical I never had much luck with the combiner y connectors but with your small panels the amps will be low enough to not matter imho even combining the combiners so 2 sets combined into one set of wires will only draw about 24 amp a guess as you did not share any information on the panels, so the 10 gauge wire you used to bring in the 2 feed wires should be good. Perhaps if designed specifically the charge controllers would not be needed for lead acid as they like being held at a high voltage so all you would need is to control the ripple and a man your understanding could do that with a few electrical parts. I never thought it stopped raining long enough on the island to support solar lol Cheers

replies.author: @mikemojc
replies.text: Where I live in the States, I happen to be about 25 miles as the crow flies from an on grid nuclear electric plant. My electric cost is in the Winter: Up to 750 kWh: $.1062 , then  for over 750 kWh each is $.0714 .   The Summer Rate is a flat $.1560 / kWh.  With grid electric costs that low, I've not yet been able to financially justify building a home/shop solar system. The ROI time goes 20% or more past the expected life of the system components.

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
Yeah, that's one of the lies the environmentalists tell in order to push their false narrative. All that green energy tech done on a commercial scale does not reduce the cost of electricity, it increases it. Especially when the government mandates it.

Here in Michigan, the more turbines and solar cells go up, the more the cost of electricity goes up.

And, with unreliable energy sources such as wind and solar, you need to have gas and coal plants ready to come on line to take over the job when the wind stops blowing and the sun decides to play peek-a-boo behind the clouds.

It is much more cost effective to just stick with the fossil fuel plants until the green tech can actually live up to their hype.

When solar is installed on a home as Robert is doing, the potential of energy cost savings increases substantially because all you are paying for is the equipment. You're only concerned with your personal energy usage, and if you find it necessary, you can adjust your energy usage for when power is more readily available. 

Expecting people to adjust their energy usage on a commercial scale is a plan designed to fail. My electric company suggested that we shift our electricity usage to a cheaper time since they now have two tear energy costs. 

The problem here is, if I shift my energy usage which is primarily the AC, I would be forced to sit in the sweltering heat of 90+ degree F. temps due to the sun shining directly into my Apartment from 12-9pm.

Basically, the environmentalists are demanding that we die of heat stroke to save the planet, when that energy will then be utilized by the big tech giants for their AI data centers and systems.

I have no intentions of capitulating to an enemies demands that I take actions to save the planet, when that same enemy is planning on increasing his energy usage, to my detriment, and to no benefit for the planet.

If you have the cash flow to buy solar panels and install them on your home, go for it. You'll end up saving money on energy. Just don't be expect us low income apartment dwellers, to do the same. If the apartment complex wants to enjoy the benefits of solar energy, they can buy the things themselves.

replies.author: @johnnodge4327
replies.text: The UK grid cost is closely aligned with the price of natural gas. I'm hoping that as more renewable energy comes on line, the price will drop, as renewable electricity is very much cheaper than a gas fueled grid.
